meta.preliminary: This is a preliminary agenda. Subject to change
meta.footerLeft: AI / DATA & INSIGHT PRIVATE CAPITAL BREAKFAST 2026  ·  PRELIMINARY AGENDA  ·  18 NOV 2026
meta.speakersSub: Senior voices from past CapLink × PE150 events  ·  AI panels and Operating Partner panels  ·  London 2025 / 2026  ·  New York 2025 / 2026

paras: The AI / Data & Insight Private Capital Breakfast is a senior-level, half-day forum designed for portfolio company executives, Data and AI leaders, operating partners, and PE deal teams. Built around the "Humans and AI" operating model, the event explores how AI is materially reshaping the private equity lifecycle, from sourcing and diligence through to portfolio value creation.

Bringing together 150 senior decision-makers in London, the breakfast provides a focused environment for practitioner-led insights on what is actually working inside operating businesses, and how PE partners can accelerate that work.

The programme features three panels covering the full PE-AI lifecycle: building the AI operating system inside portfolio companies, AI in diligence and Day-100 handoffs, and scaling AI-driven value creation across the portfolio.
We are grateful to our partners for their support and look forward to welcoming you to London.

speakersList.name: John Kelleher
speakersList.title: Managing Partner, Global Head of Operations

speakersList.name: Cameron Hulett
speakersList.title: Managing Director, Head of AI & Digital

speakersList.name: Jesse Thomas
speakersList.title: Senior Director, Head of Data Science

sessions.title: AI Into Value Creation: From Analytics to Portfolio EBITDA
sessions.desc: A panel combining AI-powered analytics and post-deal portfolio value creation: how funds move from dashboards and experimentation to repeatable AI playbooks that improve revenue, margin, productivity, and management decision quality across portfolio companies. Discussion themes: The Capability Stack — from descriptive dashboards to predictive models, prescriptive recommendations, and embedded AI workflows; Picking Battles — building scalable playbooks across portfolio companies while adapting to sector-specific context; and Humans AND AI — what the operating model actually looks like when both are pulling weight, not when one replaces the other.

sessions.title: Smarter Deals, Better Handoffs: AI in Diligence and Day-100
sessions.desc: How AI is reshaping pre-deal diligence, and how those insights carry into the first hundred days. Deal teams and operators on what survives the handoff, what gets lost, and how to make value creation planning start before signing, not after. Discussion themes: Diligence Beyond the Data Room — how AI is changing what deal teams actually look at, and where human judgement still moves the needle; Surviving the Handoff — what gets lost between IC approval and Day One, and how operators are pulling diligence work forward into value creation; and A Day-100 Plan with Teeth — from thesis to operating cadence in under fourteen weeks, with AI doing the heavy lifting on diagnostics.

tables.topic: Where Does AI Actually Create Enterprise Value?
What LPs Want to Hear About AI (and What They Don't)

sessions.title: OpenAI Deployment Company: Enterprise AI Deployment, Barriers to Entry & Market Structure
sessions.desc: A timely debate on OpenAI's new deployment-company model and what it signals for PE-backed enterprise AI adoption: whether the emerging advantage is in models, data, compute, distribution, implementation talent, or the ability to integrate AI deeply into operating workflows. Discussion themes: What DeployCo Changes — from selling models and software to deploying AI as an embedded transformation capability; Barriers to Entry — proprietary model access, compute scale, enterprise distribution, implementation talent, workflow data, and integration know-how; and The Competition Debate — could vertically integrated model-plus-deployment platforms create lock-in, privileged access, or distribution advantages, or will open-source models, multi-model architectures, and systems integrators keep the market competitive?
